title: Plaintext Gemini key persists, not erased by Reset
detail: geminiApiKey plaintext in millos-ai-config; Reset clears 5 of 16 keys, leaves it. GDPR erasure gap.
fix: Clear-all millos keys or Forget API key button.
_area: L3 Privacy · Local data + third-party egress + tracking

title: Undisclosed Gemini+Fonts egress; jsdelivr CSP unused; no trackers
detail: No Gemini egress notice (aiEngine.ts:3437). Google Fonts leak IP. Unused jsdelivr CSP. No trackers/cookies.
fix: Add Gemini notice; self-host fonts; remove jsdelivr from CSP.
_area: L3 Privacy · Local data + third-party egress + tracking

title: WebRTC exposes every peer's public IP address to untrusted peers with no consent or disclosure
detail: PEERJS_CONFIG passes no `config`/`iceServers`, so PeerJS falls back to its built-in default ICE servers (third-party): `const PEERJS_CONFIG = { debug: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? 2 : 0 };`. WebRTC connection establishment inherently exchanges ICE candidates that reveal each participant's public IP address (often local network IPs too) to every other peer in the room. The task states peers are untrusted, so every participant learns every other participant's IP. An IP address is personal data under GDPR (Recital 30 / Breyer C-582/14). It is disclosed with zero consent flow, zero disclosure, and no privacy policy anywhere in the app. This is the strongest GDPR exposure and does not depend on PeerJS broker internals.
fix: Owner decision required. Mitigations: (a) display a clear pre-join disclosure in MultiplayerLobby that joining a room reveals your IP address to other participants and routes signaling through a third-party broker; (b) optionally configure a TURN relay with `iceTransportPolicy: 'relay'` to hide peer IPs (cost/perf tradeoff); (c) publish a privacy policy covering this. Do not hardcode a specific STUN host as a 'fact'.

title: Signaling (peer IDs embedding room code + player name/id metadata) routed through default PeerJS cloud broker, a third party, undisclosed
detail: The default PeerJS cloud broker (no custom `host`/`path`/`key` in PEERJS_CONFIG; comment: 'Uses default PeerJS cloud server (free tier...)') handles signaling. What touches this third party is: (1) the peer IDs, which embed the room code in cleartext — `const peerId = this.config.isHost ? \`millos-${this.config.roomCode}\` : \`millos-${this.config.roomCode}-${this.config.playerId}\`;` (lines 53-55); and (2) the connection metadata `{ playerName: this.config.playerName, playerId: this.config.playerId }` sent at connect time (lines 122-125). Note: chat messages and player positions travel peer-to-peer over the encrypted DataChannel and do NOT pass through the broker — only signaling/handshake data does. Still, player name + room code reaching an undisclosed third-party processor with no DPA, consent, or notice is a GDPR transparency/lawful-basis gap.
fix: Owner decision: disclose the third-party broker in a privacy notice, and/or self-host peerjs-server (link already in the file comment) to keep signaling under the operator's control. Do not transmit the player name as broker metadata if it can be exchanged post-connection over the DataChannel instead.

title: Guessable 6-char room code + auto-FULL_STATE_SYNC discloses all players' names/colors to an uninvited peer
detail: The host peer ID is deterministic: `const hostPeerId = \`millos-${this.config.roomCode}\`;` where roomCode is a 6-char code from a 32-symbol alphabet (multiplayerStore.generateRoomCode). Anyone who guesses or enumerates a code connects to the host, and on connection the host immediately sends FULL_STATE_SYNC and then loops over every existing remote player broadcasting their name and color to the newcomer: `peerConn.send({ type: 'PLAYER_JOIN', payload: { id: existingPlayer.id, name: existingPlayer.name, color: existingPlayer.color } });` (MultiplayerManager.ts:214-225). So an uninvited peer receives the PII (display names) of everyone already in the room. No rate limiting, no room password, no host approval.
fix: Owner decision (UX/behavior change): add a host approval gate before sending PLAYER_JOIN/FULL_STATE_SYNC to a new connection, and/or a longer/secret room code or join PIN. Do not broadcast existing players' names to a peer until the host accepts them.

title: No consent gate: host auto-accepts any incoming peer and existing players never approve new participants
detail: handleIncomingConnection accepts every inbound connection with no gate: `private handleIncomingConnection(conn: DataConnection): void { const peerId = conn.peer; ...; this.setupConnectionHandlers(conn, peerId); }`. The host then broadcasts the newcomer's name to all other peers (MultiplayerManager.ts:228-238). Existing participants neither consent to, nor can refuse, a specific new peer learning their display name and receiving their avatar/position stream. There is no UI anywhere informing a user that entering a name and joining will share that name and live position with arbitrary other participants.
fix: Owner decision (UX/behavior): add an approval prompt for incoming peers and/or a one-line disclosure at name entry in MultiplayerLobby ('Your name, chat, and in-game position will be shared with other players in this room.').

title: Player-name entry has no data-sharing disclosure before broadcasting the name to peers and broker
detail: The name field collects free-text (up to 20 chars) and on Create/Join it is immediately stored and broadcast: `<input ... value={playerName} onChange={(e) => setPlayerName(e.target.value)} placeholder="Enter your name" maxLength={20} ... />` with no adjacent notice. handleCreateRoom/handleJoinRoom call setLocalPlayerName(playerName.trim()) and connect. Users may enter their real name without being told it is shared with untrusted peers (and sent as broker metadata). This is a transparency/data-minimization gap; the field also accepts arbitrary PII a user might volunteer.
fix: Owner decision (UX copy): add a short helper line under the name input noting the name is visible to other players in the room. Optionally suggest a nickname. No runtime-default behavior change required for the copy itself.

title: Other users' chat and names held in volatile memory only — good minimization, but no notice; chat is plaintext over the channel
detail: Positive note for data-minimization: the multiplayer store keeps remote players, peers, and chat in in-memory Maps/arrays with no persistence — grep confirmed no localStorage/sessionStorage/IndexedDB usage anywhere under src/multiplayer or multiplayerStore.ts. addChatMessage caps history at 100 (`chatMessages: [...state.chatMessages.slice(-99), message]`) and leaveRoom()/reset() drop it. The residual privacy point: chat messages carry `fromName` (display name) and are relayed to all peers via host re-broadcast (MultiplayerManager.handleMessage CHAT case) with no content disclosure or retention notice to participants. No GDPR storage issue, but the absence of any disclosure that chat is shared in cleartext with all room peers (and re-broadcast by the host) is worth flagging.
fix: Owner decision: include chat in the same pre-join disclosure ('chat is visible to everyone in the room'). No code change needed for the minimization aspect, which is already sound.
